Article Title: NEET protest: Rahul Gandhi, opposition wear black in Parliament day after PM residence protest- watch
Article Text:
Opposition reacts

NEW DELHI: Leader of opposition in the Lok Sabha Rahul Gandhi , along with MPs from the INDIA bloc, arrived in Parliament on Wednesday dressed in black to mark a "Black Day" over the alleged NEET irregularities and the police action against protesting students.Congress MP Priyanka Gandhi Vadra , Samajwadi Party chief Akhilesh Yadav, Trinamool Congress MP Mahua Moitra, JMM MP Mahua Maji and several other opposition leaders also wore black as they gathered at Makar Dwar to stage a protest on the third day of Monsoon session of Parliament. The MPs raised slogans demanding justice for students and the resignation of Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan over the controversy surrounding the examination.Akhilesh Yadav questioned why the government was unwilling to remove the Union education minister, asking,"What pressure is the government under that it cannot remove the minister?" Explaining the Opposition's decision to wear black in Parliament, he said,"We have come wearing black clothes. Black is the colour of protest; it is also the colour of grief."JMM MP Mahua Maji, while talking to reporters, said that the goal was to protect students, not score political points."Opposition stands united”, she said.Congress leaders Priyanka Gandhi Vadra and Mallikarjun Kharge also launched a sharp attack on the Centre during the Opposition's "Black Day" protest in Parliament.Speaking to reporters, Congress MP Priyanka Gandhi Vadra said the focus should remain on the students' demands rather than the treatment meted out to Opposition leaders. "What happened with us is irrelevant. Students have genuine demands and are asking for their rights," she said.She alleged that despite an education budget of Rs 1.4 lakh crore, the government had "waived off Rs 16 lakh crore loans for Adani and Ambani," while students continued to face repeated paper leaks. "Students have a genuine struggle and are asking for change. Paper leaks are happening repeatedly. There is nothing undemocratic about peaceful protest, but what is happening with students and in Parliament is undemocratic," she added.Congress president and leader of opposition in the Rajya Sabha Mallikarjun Kharge also accused the Centre of undermining democratic institutions. "They are functioning like a dictatorship. They do not respect MPs. They harass even those who protest as per rules," he said.Kharge further alleged that BJP-RSS workers were operating alongside the police and warned the government against attempting to intimidate the opposition.
Article Summary: Opposition leaders gathered at Makar Dwar to stage a protest on the third day of Monsoon session of Parliament. The MPs raised slogans demanding justice for students and the resignation of Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan over the controversy surrounding the examination.

Article Title: Daughter in SC, seeks return of Netaji's ashes from Japan
Article Text:
Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose ’s octogenarian Germany-based daughter, Anita Pfaff, moved Supreme Court

NEW DELHI: Three months after a failed attempt by his grandnephew, Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose's octogenarian Germany-based daughter, Anita Pfaff, moved Supreme Court Tuesday seeking a direction to govt to bring his ashes back from the Renko-Ji temple in Japan.Pfaff's petition, settled by senior advocate A M Singhvi, complained of "India's prolonged failure to take a final, reasoned and time-bound decision" to repatriate to India the "mortal remains/ashes attributed to Netaji, preserved at Renko-Ji temple" to allow the sole living direct lineal descendant to perform his last rites with dignity and finality."There is gross delay," said a bench of CJI Surya Kant and Justices Joymalya Bagchi and Vipul M Pancholi. However, it adjourned the hearing on Pfaff's petition on finding the arguing counsel absent.Ahead of the West Bengal assembly elections, a petition was filed in SC by Netaji's gr-andnephew, Ashis Ray, seeking the same direction to the Centre. Appearing for Ray, Singhvi had argued before the bench of the same three judges on March 12 that the petition had the support of Pfaff, who joined the proceedings virtually.Singhvi had said the fact that every head of state from India, over decades, had paid obeisance at Renko-Ji temple was proof that the freedom fighter's ashes were there.The CJI-led bench had said there was no doubt that he was one of the country's greatest freedom fighters, but refused to entertain Ray's plea.Justice Bagchi had said, "We respect her (Pfaff's) sentiments and we will ensure that her sentiments are translated into legal action. But she must step forward."Singhvi had withdrawn the petition. Heeding SC's suggestion, Pfaff filed the petition seeking repatriation of Netaji's ashes by India or, as an alternative, "facilitate her to repatriate the ashes".
Article Summary: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose’s octogenarian Germany-based daughter, Anita Pfaff, moved Supreme Court Tuesday. Pfaff's petition, settled by senior advocate A M Singhvi, complained of "India's prolonged failure to take a final, reasoned and time-bound decision"

Article Title: SC seeks reply from govt for 'not utilising' Rs 1,000cr green fund
Article Text:
SC asked the Centre and CPCB to provide details of how the over Rs 1,000-crore Environment Protection Fund was used

NEW DELHI: Supreme Court Tuesday asked the Centre and Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) to provide details of how the over Rs 1,000-crore Environment Protection Fund (EPF), which was set up in 2008 to compensate people affected by hazardous substances, was utilised.Expressing surprise at a PIL by Gyan Prakash alleging that no compensation had been paid from the fund, a bench headed by CJI Surya Kant also asked govt and CPCB to explain how it proposes to use the corpus built over the years.Instituted under Public Liability Act, 1991, which was enacted following the Bhopal gas tragedy , the primary purpose of EPF is to offer immediate relief to victims and those injured in accidents involving hazardous substances. Prakash said there had been many incidents of oil tankers catching fire, but those who lost their lives or were injured did not get anything from the fund.The 35-year-old law requires industries producing or handling hazardous material to mandatorily obtain public liability insurance. Apart from contributions from these industries, EPF gets funds from compensation awarded by National Green Tribunal for damage caused to the environment. The court was informed that the fund has been managed by CPCB from 2024.
Article Summary: SC asked Centre and CPCB to provide details of how the over Rs 1,000-crore Environment Protection Fund was used. PIL by Gyan Prakash alleging that no compensation had been paid from the fund. EPF was set up in 2008 to compensate people affected by hazardous substances.

Article Title: India summons Russian diplomat over 4 Indians killed in strike off Ukrainian coast
Article Text:
Four Indian nationals were killed after a commercial vessel, MV Golden Leo, came under attac.

Poll Vote & Share your view What do you think is the most important factor in protecting civilian sailors? Better safety measures Stronger international cooperation 3k+ users shared opinion today 5k+ users already voted today 3k+ users shared opinion today Share Opinion

NEW DELHI: India on Tuesday summoned Russian diplomat over the killing of four Indians on board a merchant vessel off the coast of Ukraine, reported news agency PTI citing sources.Four Indian nationals were killed after a commercial vessel, MV Golden Leo, came under attack while departing Ukraine's port of Odesa on Sunday, the Indian government confirmed. One more Indian crew member remains in critical condition.According to the government, the vessel had 17 crew members on board, including five Indian nationals, when it was attacked.India strongly condemned the strike, saying attacks on commercial shipping and civilian crew members were unacceptable.India, in a statement, said, “As per the information available, four Indian nationals have tragically lost their lives, while one is hospitalised in a critical condition. Our mission in Ukraine is closely monitoring the situation and is making every effort to extend all possible assistance to those affected.”The government also reiterated that targeting commercial vessels, endangering civilian seafarers and disrupting freedom of navigation and international trade was deplorable and should be avoided.One of those killed has been identified as 26-year-old merchant navy officer Akhil Joyan from Vellarikkundu in Kerala's Kasaragod district. His family was informed of his death by the shipping company through an email on Monday.Akhil had recently returned home on leave, got engaged and had resumed duty only a month ago.According to a Reuters report, Ukrainian officials said a Russian missile strike hit a ship carrying corn near the southern port of Odesa, killing 10 people.
Article Summary: Four Indian nationals were killed after a commercial vessel, MV Golden Leo, came under attack while departing Ukraine's port of Odesa on Sunday. One more Indian crew member remains in critical condition. India strongly condemned the strike, saying attacks on commercial shipping and civilian crew members were unacceptable.

Article Title: 'We owe students more than outrage': Education minister Pradhan slams Cong protest
Article Text: NEW DELHI: Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan on Tuesday accused leader of opposition in Lok Sabha Rahul Gandhi and Congress of "shamelessly exploiting students as political tools" following the grand-old-party's protest outside Prime Minister Narendra Modi's residence over the NEET paper leak.In a social media post on X, Pradhan said Congress chose to stage a dharna outside PM Modi's residence, causing inconvenience to the public and disregarding established security protocols."LoP Shri Rahul Gandhi and Congress continue to shamelessly exploit students as political tools to manufacture disruption during the Monsoon Session of Parliament ," the education minister said."Shri Rahul Gandhi and Congress chose to stage a dharna outside the Hon'ble Prime Minister's residence, causing inconvenience to the public and disregarding established security protocols," he added.Further, Pradhan said Congress "chose spectacle over democratic debate" despite the government conveying its readiness for a comprehensive discussion on the NEET paper leak issue."Even after the government conveyed its readiness for a comprehensive discussion, the Congress chose political spectacle over democratic debate. Their objective was never solutions for students; it was disruption for political headlines," he said."For Rahul Gandhi, this is not about students. This is about manufacturing confrontation after every genuine avenue for discussion has been opened," he added.Pradhan said the government owes students answers, reforms and accountability rather than outrage."Our government remains 100% committed to discussing NEET and addressing every genuine concern of our youth on the floor of the House. The students of India deserve far better than being treated as props in a political campaign. They deserve certainty over chaos, solutions over slogans and responsibility over disruption," the education minister said."We owe our students more than outrage. We owe them answers, reforms and accountability. That is exactly what our government remains committed to delivering," he added.
Article Summary: Rahul Gandhi and Congress of "shamelessly exploiting students as political tools" Union education minister Dharmendra Pradhan said Congress chose to stage a dharna outside PM Modi's residence. He said the government owes students answers, reforms and accountability rather than outrage.

Article Title: India hosts maiden Op Southern Readiness in Kochi, 26 nations join maritime exercise
Article Text:
The exercise brought together 254 participants from 26 countries

NEW DELHI: A four-day multinational maritime security training engagement, Operation Southern Readiness 26-2, commenced at the Southern Naval Command in Kochi on Monday. Hosted by the Indian Navy ’s Southern Naval Command, the engagement is being conducted under the aegis of the Navy-led Combined Task Force 154 (CTF 154), in partnership with Combined Maritime Forces (CMF), and has brought together 254 participants from 26 countries, supported by 74 facilitators and four international organisations, and Subject Matter Experts.The exercise marks the maiden conduct of Operation Southern Readiness in India and represents an important milestone in the growing engagement between the Indian Navy and CMF.CTF 154, CMF's dedicated Training Task Force, is currently commanded by the Indian Navy and is focused on strengthening capacity, interoperability and professional readiness of CMF partner nations through multinational maritime training.The opening day, broadly themed around the subject of ship management and special training, combined professional interaction with subject matter experts and practical exposure. A session by the UN Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) addressed maritime security threats in the Red Sea and Western Indian Ocean.Over the next three days, participants will continue with a diverse programme of professional sessions, simulator-based training and practical activities covering key aspects of contemporary maritime security. The engagement reflects the Indian Navy-CMF partnership in advancing regional capacity building, interoperability, and collective maritime security while promoting professional exchange and stronger relationships among participating nations.Conduct of Operation Southern Readiness by the Southern Naval Command further augments the role of the Indian Navy as the preferred security and training partner in the region.
Article Summary: Four-day multinational maritime security training engagement, Operation Southern Readiness 26-2, commenced at the Southern Naval Command in Kochi on Monday. The exercise brought together 254 participants from 26 countries, supported by 74 facilitators and four international organisations, and Subject Matter Experts.

Article Title: Assam flood toll rises: 21 killed in a day; over 5.6 lakh affected across 16 districts
Article Text:
A resident sits on the steps of a flood-affected house surrounded by inundated water

NEW DELHI: At least 21 people were killed in Assam in the last 24 hours as the flood situation worsened, with more than 5.64 lakh people affected across 16 districts, according to the Assam State Disaster Management Authority (ASDMA).The latest fatalities included 13 deaths in Sivasagar, five in Charaideo, two in Golaghat and one in Jorhat, the ASDMA said in its late-night bulletin.With the latest deaths, the overall flood toll this year has risen to 31.The number of affected people increased sharply from 3.63 lakh in 15 districts on Monday to over 5.64 lakh. Sivasagar remained the worst-hit district, with 3.59 lakh people affected, followed by Jorhat (87,662) and Charaideo (72,646).A total of 872 villages across 44 revenue circles remained inundated, while 71 relief camps sheltering 12,284 displaced people and 202 relief distribution centres are currently operational.Chief minister Himanta Biswa Sarma said he would visit the worst-affected districts on Wednesday to review relief and rescue operations."The recent #AssamFloods, caused by the cloudburst upstream, have brought immense hardship to our people.Tomorrow I will be visiting flood-affected areas and relief camps... to review the relief operations," Sarma said in a post on X.Several rivers, including the Dikhou, Disang and Brahmaputra, continued to flow above the danger mark or highest flood level at multiple locations. Rescue operations are being carried out by the Air Force, NDRF, SDRF and Fire and Emergency Services.Train services remained disrupted for the second consecutive day after floodwaters submerged railway tracks in parts of Sivasagar district. The Northeast Frontier Railway has cancelled, diverted or short-terminated several trains and is operating special services for stranded passengers.The Department of Telecommunications has also activated the Intra-Circle Roaming (ICR) facility in Sivasagar, Charaideo and Jorhat until July 24 to ensure uninterrupted mobile connectivity in the flood-hit areas.
Article Summary: Latest fatalities included 13 deaths in Sivasagar, five in Charaideo, two in Golaghat and one in Jorhat. With the latest deaths, the overall flood toll this year has risen to 31. Chief minister Himanta Biswa Sarma will visit worst- affected districts on Wednesday.

Article Title: 50% quota for ex-Agniveers in CAPF, Assam Rifles posts: MHA
Article Text:
CRPF introduced a similar amendment in recruitment rules last year

NEW DELHI: Former Agniveers will get 50% reservation in posts of constable (general duty) and rifleman in the Central Armed Police Forces (CAPFs) and Assam Rifles, the home ministry told Lok Sabha on Tuesday.In reply to a written question, junior home minister Nityanand Rai said govt has created a new category of 'ex-Agniveer' in the aforementioned posts with the provision of 50% vacancies, a three-year relaxation in the prescribed upper age limit and an age relaxation of five years beyond the prescribed age limit for candidates from the first batch of ex-Agniveers.Apart from this, he added, ex-Agniveers applying to CAPF constable (general duty) and rifleman posts will be exempted from the physical standards test, physical efficiency test and written examination. "A dedicated ex-Agniveer wing under the ministry of home affairs has been established for the purpose of coordinating further progression of ex-Agniveers," said Rai.The first batch of Agniveers have not yet completed their four-year tenure. A notification issued by home ministry in Dec 2025 had amended the Border Security Force, General Duty Cadre (Non-Gazetted) Recruitment Rules, 2015, to increase the quota for ex-Agniveers in constable (general duty) posts to 50% from 10%."By direct recruitment (including 50%), vacancies shall be reserved for ex-Agniveers in every recruitment year, 10% for ex-servicemen and up to 3% for combatised constable (tradesmen) in absorption of annual vacancy," the notification had stated. The CRPF introduced a similar amendment in recruitment rules in May last year.
Article Summary: Former Agniveers will get 50% reservation in posts of constable (general duty) and rifleman in the Central Armed Police Forces (CAPFs) and Assam Rifles, the home ministry told Lok Sabha on Tuesday. The CRPF introduced a similar amendment in recruitment rules last year.

Article Title: SC reluctant to take up PIL on paper leaks; asks what and how much can courts do
Article Text:
The bench adjourned the hearing to next month, observing that it was already seized of the NEET-UG paper leak case.

Poll Vote & Share your view What should be the priority to protect students' rights in competitive exams? Enhanced security measures Strict action against offenders Better transparency in exam processes 3k+ users shared opinion today 5k+ users already voted today 3k+ users shared opinion today Share Opinion

NEW DELHI: Supreme Court Tuesday expressed reluctance to entertain a PIL seeking its intervention to curb question paper leaks in competitive examinations and to protect students' interests, saying the scope of the plea was too vast."What can the court do? How much the court can do," a bench of Justices P S Narasimha and Alok Aradhe said when senior advocate Vikas Singh urged it to examine the plea, saying that more than 2.5 crore students had suffered due to question paper leaks in NEET and several other examinations.The bench, however, adjourned the hearing to next month, observing that it was already seized of the NEET-UG paper leak case.The court was hearing a petition by advocate Ashwini Upadhyay, who said repeated paper leaks leave students in dire straits. "It takes a toll on students to face criticism over their life choices, such as spending an entire year preparing for an examination that eventually stands vitiated by a paper leak. The financial distress caused to students also has a direct bearing on their dignity," the petition said."The State owes a heightened duty of care to students while conducting examinations. Students place their trust in the system and invest years of hard work and substantial sums on coaching, study materials, commuting, rent and other expenses. The State effectively induces students and their families to alter their position based on the assurance that examinations will be conducted as scheduled. When a paper is leaked, those expectations are shattered. Therefore, the State is violating students' right to equality under Article 14 of the Constitution," it added.
Article Summary: The bench adjourned the hearing to next month, observing that it was already seized of the NEET-UG paper leak case. The court was hearing a petition by advocate Ashwini Upadhyay, who said repeated paper leaks leave students in dire straits. "It takes a toll on students to face criticism over their life choices," petition said.

Article Title: Govt restores WPI linking factor; toll rates on national highways rise marginally
Article Text:
Toll operators said the hike is in the range of 2-5%

NEW DELHI: Toll charges on National Highways (NHs) and expressways have marginally increased at several toll plazas across the country, particularly for commercial and heavy vehicles, following a Delhi High Court order restoring the use of a linking factor to convert the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) series for revising user fees.Toll operators said the hike is in the range of 2-5% depending on vehicle category, and in most cases, there is no rise in toll charge for private cars. “This doesn’t impact cars that have FASTag Annual Pass. Even for those which don’t have the pass, there is hardly any increase. Govt has only reinstated the linking factor for toll revision,” said an official.Govt revises the toll charge every April. However, following an observation by Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) on govt’s linking factor due to change in base year of WPI from 2004-05 to 2011-12 for toll fee revision, road transport ministry, in Sep 2025, had used the linking factor at older rate (2004-5). This resulted in reduction of toll charge, and even the revision in fee in April this year happened at the older rate.But highway operators challenged the ministry's order in Delhi HC, and the court ruled in their favour. Subsequently, complying with the court order, the ministry on June 29 decided to use the 2011-12 linking factor. “So, the hike of April 2025 has been reinstated, and the rates have also been revised from this April,” said a toll operator.
Article Summary: Toll operators said the hike is in the range of 2-5% depending on vehicle category. In most cases, there is no rise in toll charge for private cars. Delhi High Court order restoring the use of a linking factor to convert the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) series.

Article Title: Centre sets up cybercrime cells in Jamtara, Mewat; over Rs 11,000 crore saved from fraud
Article Text:
Representative image

NEW DELHI: Jamtara in Jharkhand and Mewat in Haryana, which have emerged as hubs of cyber fraud , are among the seven locations in the country where the Indian Cyber Crime Coordination Centre (I4C) has set up joint cybercrime coordination teams (JCCTs) for inter-state synergy in tracking and investigation of cyber crimes.Sharing this information on Tuesday in reply to a written question in the Lok Sabha, junior home minister Bandi Sanjay Kumar said the 7 JCCTs -- working in ‘cybercrime hotspots’ and areas having multi-jurisdictional issues -- in Jamtara, Mewat, Hyderabad, Ahmedabad, Chandigarh, Visakhapatnam and Guwahati coordinate law enforcement actions across a set of states./UTs. The constituent state police share information through the JCCT mechanism on name, residence and mobile number of cyber criminals as well as case details, facilitating expeditious action.Kumar informed that the citizen financial cyber fraud reporting and management system (CFCFRMS), launched by I4C in 2021, has saved over Rs 11,158 crore so far from being siphoned by fraudsters in action taken on 32.8 lakh complaints. Also, nearly 66 lakh financial fraud complaints were received on the helpline ‘1930’ between 2021 and 2025, involving a total amount of over Rs 55,000 crore. An amount of Rs 8,189 crore was marked as lien and 1.9 lakh FIRs registered.Elaborating on I4C’s ‘Pratibimb’ module -- which maps the locations of criminals and cybercrime infrastructure and facilitates techno-legal assistance to law enforcement agencies -- the minister said it has led to the arrest of over 29,837 accused and facilitated over 2.3 lakh cyber investigation assistance requests.
Article Summary: The Indian Cyber Crime Coordination Centre (I4C) has set up joint cybercrime coordination teams (JCCTs) for inter-state synergy in tracking and investigation of cyber crimes. The constituent state police share information through the JCCT mechanism on name, residence and mobile number of cyber criminals.

Article Title: Arunachal's draft electoral roll shrinks by nearly 20% after SIR
Article Text:
Election Commission

NEW DELHI: Arunachal Pradesh’s electorate has shrunk by almost one-fifth in the draft electoral roll published on Tuesday as part of the special intensive revision (SIR) exercise.Only 80.9% electors in the border state submitted their enumeration forms, while the remaining 19.1% were marked as permanently shifted, absent, dead or enrolled at more than one place.The draft roll of the state — which borders China — published on Tuesday has a total 7.18 lakh electors, as against 8.9 lakh electors on June 2, 2026, when SIR Phase 3 was announced. As many as 78,980 electors (8.9%) were marked as permanently shifted, 54,678 (6.2%) as absent or untraceable, 25,573 as dead (2.9%) and 5,281 (0.6%) as others (not willing to be enrolled for some reason of other).As per the Arunachal chief electoral officer (CEO), genuine electors can still be added back to the rolls during the claims and objection period from July 21 to August 20. These claims/objections will be disposed of by Septmber 18 and the final rolls of the state published on September 22.
Article Summary: Only 80.9% electors in the border state submitted their enumeration forms. The remaining 19.1% were marked as permanently shifted, absent, dead or enrolled at more than one place. The draft roll of the state has a total 7.18 lakh electors, as against 8.9 lakh electors on June 2, 2026.

Article Title: 'Minor can be tried as adult if crime qualifies as heinous offence': Supreme Court
Article Text:
Supreme Court

(With PTI inputs)

NEW DELHI: The Supreme Court on Tuesday held that a juvenile accused of murder can be tried as an adult if the crime qualifies as a "heinous offence" under the Juvenile Justice Act.The judgement, aimed at clarifying the legal framework for trying minors as adults, held that murder under Section 302 of the IPC must be classified as a "heinous offence" under the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015."The offence punishable under Section 302 of the IPC, which prescribes punishment of 'death or imprisonment for life', carries life imprisonment as its minimum punishment. It would therefore be categorized as a 'heinous offence'," the judgement authored by Justice Pardiwala stated.The juvenile's counsel had argued that since Section 302 of the IPC prescribes "death or imprisonment for life" but does not explicitly mention the word "minimum", murder should be categorised as a "serious offence" rather than a "heinous" one."Life imprisonment is, by necessary implication, the minimum punishment for murder since courts cannot impose a sentence below life imprisonment upon conviction under Section 302," the order held.The legal battle centred on a technicality in sentencing definitions. Under the Juvenile Justice Act, "heinous offences" are those that carry a minimum imprisonment term of seven years or more.The Patna High Court had held that the appellant needed to be tried as an adult and directed the Juvenile Justice Board to transfer the case to a regular court.The Juvenile Justice Board had initially decided that the child should be tried by the Board itself, but the appellate sessions court overturned the decision. The high court later upheld the order allowing the juvenile to be tried as an adult, following which the accused approached the Supreme Court.
Article Summary: A juvenile accused of murder can be tried as an adult if the crime qualifies as a "heinous offence" under the Juvenile Justice Act. The legal battle centred on a technicality in sentencing definitions. The juvenile's counsel had argued that since Section 302 of the IPC prescribes "death or imprisonment for life" but does not explicitly mention the word "minimum", murder should be categorised as a'serious offence'

Article Title: 70% of electricity consumed during April-June quarter came from thermal energy
Article Text:
Representational image

NEW DELHI: Even as the share of renewable energy (RE) in the power mix has steadily increased, thermal power continues to remain the mainstay, with 70% of the electricity consumed in the country during the April-June quarter coming from coal-based plants.During the non-solar peak-demand hours, the share of coal-based generation reached a maximum of 75%, minister of state for power Shripad Yesso Naik informed Rajya Sabha.The share of renewable energy has increased significantly over the last few years, with solar, wind, nuclear and hydro together accounting for over 54% of India’s total installed capacity of 548.8 gigawatts. Wind and solar energy set a record earlier this month, generating more than 100 GW together. While the high penetration of variable RE is a reason to rejoice, it is coal-based thermal power that carries the bulk of the load when solar energy is not available and wind intensity drops.With 230.8 GW of installed capacity, the maximum generation achieved from coal- and lignite-based thermal power plants was about 188.8 GW in the first quarter of FY27. While 9.5 GW of thermal capacity was added in 2025-26, the minister informed that 2.3 GW had been commissioned during the current fiscal.The minister also said that, as on July 12, 42.8 million tonne of coal stock was available at thermal power plants, sufficient for 14 days at an 85% plant load factor. He added that sufficient coal was being received by thermal power plants to meet the daily requirement and that a joint committee of the ministries of power, railways and coal was monitoring supplies on a daily basis.Responding to a separate question, the minister informed Parliament that 11 pumped storage projects with an aggregate installed capacity of 15.8 GW were under construction, while another six, with an aggregate installed capacity of 8.2 GW, had received concurrence from the Central Electricity Authority.
Article Summary: 70% of the electricity consumed in the country during the April-June quarter came from coal-based plants. The share of renewable energy has increased significantly over the last few years. Wind and solar energy set a record earlier this month, generating more than 100 GW together.

Article Title: Kremlin says 'in touch' with New Delhi after 4 Indians killed in Ukraine ship strike
Article Text: Russia on Tuesday said it is "in touch" with its partners in New Delhi after four Indians were killed on board a merchant vessel off the coast of Ukraine.This comes after Kyiv claimed that Russia hit the Golden Leo—a Guinea-Bissau-flagged ship manned by a crew from India and Syria—with three cruise missiles on Sunday, killing 10 people.Earlier in the day, New Delhi summoned the Russian chargé d'affaires over the incident, news agency PTI reported, citing sources.The Ministry of External Affairs strongly condemned the strike, saying attacks on commercial shipping and civilian crew members were unacceptable.The MEA, in a statement, said, "As per the information available, four Indian nationals have tragically lost their lives, while one is hospitalised in a critical condition. Our mission in Ukraine is closely monitoring the situation and is making every effort to extend all possible assistance to those affected."The government also reiterated that targeting commercial vessels, endangering civilian seafarers, and disrupting freedom of navigation and international trade were deplorable and should be avoided.One of those killed has been identified as 26-year-old merchant navy officer Akhil Joyan from Vellarikkundu in Kerala's Kasaragod district.His family was informed of his death by the shipping company through an email on Monday.Akhil had recently returned home on leave, got engaged, and resumed duty only a month ago.According to a Reuters report, Ukrainian officials said a Russian missile strike hit a ship carrying corn near the southern port of Odesa, killing 10 people.
Article Summary: Russia says it is "in touch" with its partners in New Delhi after four Indians were killed on board a merchant vessel off the coast of Ukraine. This comes after Kyiv claimed that Russia hit the Golden Leo with three cruise missiles on Sunday, killing 10 people.

Article Title: Pakistan asks US for $10bn lifeline to shore up struggling economy: Report
Article Text:
The proposed facility would strengthen Pakistan's foreign exchange reserves

Seeking a rare US financial backstop



Economy still under pressure



Pakistan has asked the United States for a $10 billion exchange stabilisation facility in a bid to shore up its fragile economy, according to a source familiar with the matter, as Islamabad looks to ease pressure on its foreign exchange reserves and currency.The request, reported by Reuters, comes as Pakistan continues to grapple with the aftermath of its worst economic crisis in decades despite an ongoing International Monetary Fund (IMF) bailout programme. The proposed facility, if approved, would provide a significant financial cushion for the cash-strapped country while reducing its dependence on multilateral lenders.The request also follows Pakistan's role in helping broker talks during the recent US-Iran conflict, which boosted Islamabad's diplomatic standing and fuelled expectations that it could seek stronger economic backing from Washington.According to Reuters, Pakistan has asked US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to approve a Bilateral Exchange Stabilisation Support Facility worth $10 billion with a maturity period of up to five years.The proposed facility would strengthen Pakistan's foreign exchange reserves, help stabilise the currency and improve confidence in the country's financial position while it continues implementing fiscal and monetary reforms under its IMF programme.The ministry said he sought greater US support for Pakistan's return to international capital markets through higher foreign exchange reserves, stronger sovereign credit ratings and increased investment. Both sides also reaffirmed their commitment to expanding bilateral economic cooperation and advancing strategic projects.Exchange stabilisation facilities are uncommon US Treasury instruments, typically financed through the Exchange Stabilization Fund. Unlike the Federal Reserve's permanent dollar swap lines with select central banks, these arrangements provide temporary dollar liquidity, guarantees or swaps to help countries stabilise their currencies during periods of financial stress, Reuters reported.Pakistan narrowly avoided a sovereign default in 2023 after securing a $3 billion IMF standby arrangement, followed by a $7 billion Extended Fund Facility and a separate $1.3 billion climate resilience loan. However, the country's external finances remain heavily dependent on IMF support, debt rollovers and deposits from key partners including China and Saudi Arabia.Economists say Pakistan's economic difficulties stem from decades of weak policy management, persistent fiscal deficits and rapid population growth, which have constrained savings, investment and long-term growth. The country has also struggled with mounting public debt, while interest payments consume a large share of government revenue, leaving limited room for spending on health, education and infrastructure.Although IMF-backed reforms have helped stabilise the economy by restoring reserves and easing pressure on the rupee, analysts caution that Pakistan continues to face significant financing needs and a heavy external debt repayment burden. Against that backdrop, a $10 billion US-backed stabilisation facility would represent one of Islamabad's biggest attempts in recent years to secure bilateral financial support and strengthen its economic recovery.
Article Summary: Pakistan has asked the United States for a $10 billion exchange stabilisation facility in a bid to shore up its fragile economy. The proposed facility would provide a significant financial cushion for the cash-strapped country while reducing its dependence on multilateral lenders. The request comes as Pakistan continues to grapple with the aftermath of its worst economic crisis in decades.

Article Title: Despite 35 minute delay Vikram-1 keeps date with Indian space history with Mission Aagaman success
Article Text: SRIHARIKOTA: After some anxious moments when the automatic launch sequence of Vikram-1 was halted midway at just five minutes before lift-off, Skyroot Aerospace finally created history after it successfully placed India’s first privately developed orbital-class rocket into space.The seven-storey tall Vikram-1 rocket, with a lift-off weight of 45,000 kg, blasted off at 12.05 pm, a good 35 minutes behind the originally scheduled time, from the first launch pad at the Satish Dhawan Space Centre-Sriharikota Range on its maiden orbital test flight, Mission Aagaman.The vehicle completed its planned flight sequence and placed its payloads into the targeted low Earth orbit, validating key systems developed by the Hyderabad-based startup.Prime Minister Narendra Modi spoke personally with the Skyroot founders Pawan Kumar Chandana and Naga Bharath Daka on phone and congratulated the duo on the successful completion of Mission Aagaman.The PM, who sent a postcard into space abroad Vikram-1 with Vande Mataram written on it, pointed out to the Skyroot team that the feat was also momentous coming as it does when Vande Mataram is commemorating 150 years.The feat marks a major milestone for the country’s commercial launch sector and opens a new chapter at Sriharikota 46 years after ISRO’s first successfully launched India’s first launch vehicle SLV-3 into orbit.The launch came exactly 46 years after ISRO’s SLV-3E2 placed the Rohini satellite RS-1 into orbit on July 18, 1980, making India the sixth nation capable of launching satellites independently. Like SLV-3, Vikram-1 stands 22 metres tall, but its success represents a different leap: the arrival of private Indian industry in orbital launch operations.Built with an all-carbon composite structure, Vikram-1 uses in-house propulsion systems, including high-thrust solid-fuel stages and 3D-printed engines. The mission targeted a 450 km orbit at an inclination of 60 degrees and lasted just under 18 minutes.The rocket carried a mix of demonstration and symbolic payloads, including Skyroot’s SCOPE payload, SOLARAS, a 1U CubeSat from Karnataka-based Grahaa Space, Embrace, an in-orbit robotic arm demonstration from Telangana-based Cosmoserve Space, and payloads from Germany’s DCubed GmbH.It also carried a handwritten postcard from Prime Minister Narendra Modi bearing the words “Vande Mataram”, along with hundreds of letters from Skyroot’s team and supporters.In attendance at the historic launch were astronaut Shubhanshu Shukla, Andhra Pradesh IT minister Nara Lokesh, with his son in tow, in addition to ISRO chairman V Narayanan, IN-SPACe chairman Pawan Goenka and former ISRO director S Somanath.Founded in 2018 by former ISRO scientists Chandana and Daka, Skyroot had earlier made history on Nov 18, 2022 when Vikram-S became the first privately built Indian rocket to reach space on a suborbital flight. With Aagaman, the company has now moved from demonstration to orbital capability.
Article Summary: Skyroot Aerospace successfully launched India's first privately developed orbital-class rocket into space. The seven-storey tall Vikram-1 rocket blasted off at 12.05 pm, a good 35 minutes behind the originally scheduled time. Prime Minister Narendra Modi spoke personally with the Skyroot founders Pawan Kumar Chandana and Naga Bharath Daka on phone.

Article Title: Judge approves a $1.5 billion Anthropic settlement over pirated books used to train the Claude chatbot
Article Text:
A federal judge has approved a $1.5 billion copyright settlement

SAN FRANCISCO: A federal judge has approved a $1.5 billion copyright settlement in which artificial intelligence company Anthropic will pay thousands of authors about $3,000 per book after using pirated copies of their works to train its Claude chatbot.District Judge Araceli Martinez-Olguin said in a Monday ruling that the class-action settlement provides "meaningful relief" to affected authors and publishers.About 91% of the more than 482,000 books covered by the ruling have been claimed by authors or publishers who are now due payment.Plaintiff attorney Justin Nelson said in a statement that the settlement was "the largest known copyright recovery in history. We look forward to making distributions to the Class as promptly as possible."U.S. District Judge William Alsup issued the preliminary approval in San Francisco federal court last September and has since retired. Alsup had dealt the case a mixed ruling last summer, finding that training AI chatbots on copyrighted books wasn't illegal but that Anthropic wrongfully acquired millions of books through pirate websites.Anthropic's deputy general counsel, Aparna Sridhar, highlighted that ruling Friday as a landmark showing "that training AI on books is fair use under copyright law.""We are pleased that more than 91% of authors and publishers covered by the settlement have claimed their share of the payment, and we're looking forward to bringing this matter to a close," Sridhar said in a written statement.Bestselling thriller novelist Andrea Bartz first brought the suit with two other authors in 2024. It's the first major settlement in dozens of AI copyright lawsuits that are still working their way through courts.
Article Summary: Artificial intelligence company Anthropic will pay thousands of authors about $3,000 per book. Anthropic used pirated copies of their works to train its Claude chatbot. About 91% of the more than 482,000 books covered by the ruling have been claimed.

Article Title: US student visa rules are changing on September 15: Who could be affected and how
Article Text:
Trump admin introduces fixed visa stay limits for foreign students (Representative Photo)

Key rule changes

Fixed four-year admission period replaces 'Duration of Status'

Existing students will also move to the new system

Extensions will require USCIS approval

Post-study grace period cut to 30 days

Stricter rules on academic changes

Why are universities asking students to return before September 15?

Why are education groups concerned?

The United States has overhauled its student visa framework, replacing the decades-old "Duration of Status" (D/S) system with fixed periods of admission for F-1 students and J-1 exchange visitors.According to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), the changes are aimed at curbing visa abuse, strengthening security checks and bringing greater federal oversight to international students.The new rules, which take effect on September 15, introduce a maximum four-year stay for most students, shorten the post-study grace period and require government approval for extensions beyond the authorised stay.The changes are particularly significant for Indian students, who now make up the largest international student community in the US.More than 3.6 lakh Indians were enrolled in American universities during the 2024-25 academic year, many of them pursuing master's, doctoral and STEM programmes that often extend beyond four years.While the DHS says the new framework will improve monitoring of foreign students, universities and higher education groups have warned that the additional paperwork and tighter timelines could create uncertainty for students pursuing longer academic programmes.Under the new rules, F-1 students and J-1 exchange visitors will no longer be allowed to remain in the US for the duration of their academic programme without a fixed end date.Instead, they will be admitted for the length of their programme, subject to a maximum period of four years. Students whose courses extend beyond that period will have to seek approval from US Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) to continue their studies.International students already in the US under the old "Duration of Status" framework will automatically transition to the new rules. Their authorised stay will also be subject to the four-year limit from the rule's effective date.Students who need additional time to complete their degree will have to file an Extension of Stay application with USCIS. The process includes biometrics, background checks and additional scrutiny, shifting authority from universities to the federal government.The grace period after completing studies has been reduced from 60 days to 30 days. During this period, students must either leave the US, transfer to another institution, change visa status or take steps related to Optional Practical Training (OPT), where applicable.The rule also introduces tighter controls on changing majors, extending academic programmes and pursuing another degree, requiring greater government oversight than under the previous system.Several universities, including Columbia University, have advised international students currently outside the US to return before the new rules come into force on September 15.Institutions say doing so could help students avoid complications as the immigration system transitions from the old "Duration of Status" framework to the new fixed-period admission model.The DHS says the new framework will restore integrity to the immigration system and prevent misuse of student visas.However, organisations such as NAFSA and the Foundation for India and Indian Diaspora Studies (FIIDS) argue the changes could disproportionately affect students enrolled in longer programmes, including PhDs and research-intensive STEM courses.They also warn that additional government approvals could increase uncertainty, delay research and make the US a less attractive destination for international talent.
Article Summary: The new rules take effect on September 15. They introduce a maximum four-year stay for most students. They also shorten the post-study grace period and require government approval for extensions. The changes are particularly significant for Indian students, who now make up the largest international student community in the US.

Article Title: Larry Ellison, Ken Griffin, Dace Brown Stubbs and other billionaires are buying neighboring houses using 'methods' called 'Landmaxxing', 'Defensive buying' and ...
Article Text:
Larry Ellison

How and why Larry Elisson, Jeff Bezos and other billionaires are expanding their compounds



Larry Ellison and several other billionaires may be increasingly buying neighbouring homes and land to create larger private compounds. A report claims that the Oracle founder, hedge fund billionaire Ken Griffin, Jack Daniel's heiress Dace Brown Stubbs and several other wealthy buyers are doing this. According to Coldwell Banker's midyear luxury market report, the trend has given rise to terms such as "Landmaxxing", which means acquiring additional land to expand a property, and "defensive buying," where buyers purchase adjacent land to preserve privacy or protect views.Citing the report, The Wall Street Journal noted that searches for US. luxury properties doubled in the first five months of 2026 compared to the same period in 2025. Searches for buildable land jumped 97% year-over-year, and searches for private islands and estates more than doubled, indicating rising demand for larger, multi-parcel properties.Among the buyers highlighted in the report is Citadel founder Ken Griffin, who has spent more than $450 million assembling a 27-acre compound in Palm Beach. Amazon founder Jeff Bezos has purchased properties worth more than $230 million on Miami's Indian Creek Island, while Oracle founder Larry Ellison has continued acquiring neighbouring properties in Malibu, California, Incline Village, Nevada, and Manalapan, Florida.A real estate agent named Danny Hertzberg working for Coldwell Banker Realty said many buyers now see adjacent properties as both lifestyle assets and long-term investments."This is more sought-after than the 40,000-square-foot house. For some people, it is privacy and security. For others, it is a hedge against inflation,” he said.He added that neighbouring property owners are often the first buyers approached when a home goes on sale because they are willing to pay more to expand their holdings.Real estate agents say buyers are using neighbouring properties for a range of purposes, including guest accommodation, staff housing, gardens, boating facilities, gyms, spas and sports courts.Venture capitalist Benjamin Ling expanded his holdings on Miami Beach's Palm Island by purchasing the waterfront property next to his home. Investor Ibrahim Al-Rashid also bought two neighbouring homes on Hibiscus Island, later building a guesthouse with a gym, spa and a man-made fish pond alongside his primary residence.Businessman Patrick Dovigi purchased three nearby parcels last year, with plans to add a gym, spa and cabana next to his waterfront home, along with a padel court on another lot across the street.Some buyers are also engaging in what Hertzberg described as "defensive buying," purchasing adjacent land to create a buffer between themselves and future developments.Dace Brown Stubbs, whose family founded the company behind Jack Daniel's, said she expanded her Colorado ranch after concerns that new construction could alter the surrounding landscape."I hated the thought of a giant house or two being built on these beautiful fields,” she said.Stubbs initially purchased a 14.26-acre ranch and later spent an additional $1.15 million over several years to acquire another 40 acres to preserve the property's views.The report also highlighted WeatherTech founder David MacNeil, who has acquired multiple adjacent parcels in Manalapan, Florida. One nearby parcel is also being purchased by Larry Ellison, further expanding the Oracle founder's existing compound in the area.According to Coldwell Banker, average luxury single-family home sales volume increased 2.8% year over year between January and May, while the median luxury home price rose 4.7% to $1.83 million. The brokerage said the luxury market has continued to be driven largely by cash buyers and long-term wealth creation rather than mortgage rates.
Article Summary: Larry Ellison, Ken Griffin, Jeff Bezos and others are buying neighbouring homes and land to create larger private compounds. The trend has given rise to terms such as "Landmaxxing," which means acquiring additional land to expand a property, and "defensive buying," where buyers purchase adjacent land to preserve privacy or protect views. Searches for US luxury properties doubled in the first five months of 2026 compared to the same period in 2025.

Article Title: Scientists heard bird sounds coming from panels of a floating Solar Farm in Netherlands proving birds could find homes beneath …
Article Text:
Representative image

Birds and aquatic life found around floating solar farm

Bird sounds suggest possible nesting under solar panels

A floating solar farm in the Netherlands may be doing more than just producing clean electricity. Scientists say they heard bird sounds coming from beneath the solar panels, a possibility that birds could be nesting under the structure. The finding comes from a study by researchers that looked at how floating solar farms affect local wildlife. As part of the study, researchers found that underwater shelters installed below the panels attracted hundreds of fish and thousands of small aquatic creatures over three years. The results suggest that, with the right design, floating solar farms could support biodiversity instead of harming local ecosystems.The research focused on the Bomhofsplas Solar Farm, a floating solar project built on a lake in the Netherlands. Between 2020 and 2023, researchers installed 20 underwater "Biohuts", cage-like structures beneath different parts of the solar farm, designed to provide shelter and food for small fish while helping scientists understand how floating solar farms affect aquatic ecosystems.By the end of the study, the Biohuts had attracted more than 400 fish and nearly 2,000 invertebrates, including mussels and sponges.Researchers said the structures gradually developed into small ecosystems that supported life higher up the food chain.The study also found signs that birds may be using the floating solar farm. In a 2021 paper published in the journal Sustainability, researchers reported hearing bird sounds beneath the solar panels, suggesting that nests could be present under the floating structures.Researchers believe the latest findings show that carefully designed floating solar farms could reduce some of those impacts while creating new habitats for wildlife.Besides supporting wildlife, floating solar panels also provide shade that can help reduce water evaporation. The water below also naturally cools the panels, which can improve their performance.
Article Summary: Researchers found that underwater shelters installed below the panels attracted hundreds of fish and thousands of small aquatic creatures over three years. The results suggest that, with the right design, floating solar farms could support biodiversity instead of harming local ecosystems. The study also found signs that birds may be using the floating solar farm.

Article Title: A memory shortage should scare a brand Lava's size, but MD Sunil Raina says it's an opening
Article Text:
Sunil Raina, Managing Director at Lava

A global memory shortage is pushing component costs up across the phone industry, squeezing exactly the mid-range tier where Lava lives. It should be a threat. Sunil Raina, Lava's managing director, has decided to treat it as an opening.His logic on the shortage is contrarian but clean. "The issue today is supply chain—component availability. Price is not the issue, because everybody is increasing prices. It's at parity," he says. A phone that sold at 10,000 rupees lands at 15,000; every brand moves together; the market shrinks a little. What was a 15,000-rupee spec sheet earlier now sits at 25,000 or 30,000. Nobody is spared, so nobody is undercut.That parity is where Raina sees room. "In our case, because our market share is not very high, the macro doesn't matter much," he says. "You're taking share from somebody." A brand sitting at two to three percent doesn't need the whole market to grow—it needs to pull buyers off someone bigger. Fifty percent growth is a mountain for the market and a molehill for Lava. Seventeen years in the business, he argues, has bought the supply-chain relationships to actually secure parts when they're scarce, and supply—not demand—is the only real constraint."If we get the supply, we'll do 50 percent or more. The first three months are already 40, 50 percent over last year."That discipline extends to what Lava won't do: chase the price umbrella upward faster than it has to. "I can't expect people to pay 250 just because I'm a new brand," Raina says of how he approaches any market—hold the spec-for-spec value even when costs are climbing, because the moment you break parity is the moment a rival takes the buyer back.It's a coherent bet, and an exposed one. The smallness that has always capped Lava is now the thing it's counting on—a low base that makes steep growth easier to claim, and a shortage that flattens the field just enough for a minor player to move. Whether that's strategy or the only move available is, as ever with Lava, a question of who you ask.
Article Summary: A global memory shortage is pushing component costs up across the phone industry. Sunil Raina, Lava's managing director, has decided to treat it as an opening. A phone that sold at 10,000 rupees lands at 15,000; every brand moves together.

Article Title: It's no country for freshers as IT firms revisit campus hiring playbook
Article Text:
Representative image

Bengaluru:It's no country for freshers.Campus offers are being delayed, onboarding schedules are being pushed back, and in some cases, roles are quietly withdrawn. Joining dates are increasingly tied to shifting business demand, leaving thousands of engineering graduates waiting months—sometimes over a year—for jobs they once assumed were guaranteed. For decades, a campus offer from an IT company came with an unwritten promise: graduate, join training and begin work. That certainty is beginning to disappear.The language companies are using reflects this new reality.IBM recently wrote to candidates waiting for an offer: "While we are trying to get you an early joining, there needs to be appropriate available demand to map you to that project. Once we have an update on the tentative date, our team will get in touch with you and initiate the offer process."At Accenture, after candidates applied for its Custom Software Engineer role, the company informed them: "Due to certain recent developments, we are no longer recruiting for this role." It added that candidates would be contacted if suitable openings matching their profile became available.At Oracle Financial Services Software (OFSS), candidates say offer letters have been delayed.At Cognizant, some GenC recruits have received repeated emails stating their joining date would depend on business requirements, successful background verification and delays linked to the EPFO's UAN portal.One email stated: "Please note that the EPFO portal is currently under maintenance and is expected to be resolved today. Based on the completion of your BGV formalities, the concerned team is working on revising your date of joining. We will keep you informed of any further updates."Another read: "Due to downtime on the UAN website, there may be a delay in processing. Your Date of Joining (DOJ) will be finalized based on business requirements and is subject to the successful completion of the Background Verification (BGV) process. We will keep you informed of further updates. Thank you for your patience and understanding."While administrative issues have contributed to some delays, the repeated references to "business requirements" illustrate how onboarding schedules have become increasingly fluid.The uncertainty has prompted freshers to create their own tracking tools. A 2025 Wipro recruit, frustrated by months of silence over onboarding, launched OnboardWatch, a crowdsourced website where candidates anonymously report whether their offers have been delayed, honoured or revoked.The anxiety comes amid a broader reset in the IT industry's hiring model.India's five largest IT services companies—TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCLTech and Tech Mahindra—collectively reduced their workforce by 6,981 employees in FY26, reversing net additions of 12,718 in FY25. TCS accounted for the biggest reduction, trimming 23,460 employees, while Tech Mahindra cut 1,108.Even as hiring slows at large IT firms, the nature of demand is changing. Nasscom estimates the technology industry's workforce grew by 1.35 lakh to 5.9 million in 2026, only marginally higher than the previous year's addition. Much of the incremental hiring is now coming from global capability centres (GCCs).Companies also say freshers require longer training before they become billable as enterprise technology becomes more complex.Namratha Dharshan, chief business leader at ISG, said opportunities for freshers remain, but employer expectations have changed. "Employers are increasingly prioritising specialised and deep-tech skills, with growing demand for talent that can work effectively with AI and emerging technologies rather than broad-based, generalist capabilities," she said.Phil Fersht, CEO of HFS Research, said the slowdown in campus onboarding reflects a structural shift in the industry's talent model. "Over the next three to five years, the traditional pyramid hiring model that powered India's IT services industry for decades will flatten considerably. Companies will recruit fewer people for generic delivery roles, but demand will increase for graduates with AI literacy, business domain knowledge, data engineering, cybersecurity and industry-specific expertise," he said.Even companies that continue to hire insist recruitment remains demand-led. TCS, which added more than 9,000 employees in the June quarter, says it continues to hire both experienced professionals and fresh graduates.Emails sent to IBM, Accenture, Oracle and Wipro seeking comment did not elicit a response till press time. Cognizant, however, said it remains committed to honouring all offers.Getting the offer, it seems, is no longer the same as getting the job.
Article Summary: Joining dates are increasingly tied to shifting business demand. The uncertainty has prompted freshers to create their own tracking tools. India's five largest IT services companies collectively reduced their workforce by 6,981 employees in FY26, reversing net additions of 12,718 in FY25.

Article Title: Iran war risk: JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon warns of oil shocks, sticky inflation and higher interest rates
Article Text: JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon has warned that the ongoing war in Iran could trigger oil and commodity price shocks, keeping inflation elevated and pushing interest rates higher than current market expectations, Reuters reported.The warning came in his annual letter to shareholders, a day after US President Donald Trump escalated pressure on Iran by threatening to target key infrastructure if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened."Now, because of the war in Iran, we additionally face the potential for significant ongoing oil and commodity price shocks, along with the reshaping of global supply chains, which may lead to stickier inflation and ultimately higher interest rates than markets currently expect," Dimon said, news agency Reuters quoted as saying.Dimon, who has led JPMorgan for two decades, also highlighted broader geopolitical risks, including the war in Ukraine, tensions in the Middle East and friction with China."The challenges we all face are significant," he added.He said it remains uncertain whether the Iran war will achieve US objectives, while warning that nuclear proliferation remains the biggest risk linked to Iran.Markets have already begun pricing in these risks, with expectations of interest rate cuts this year largely fading amid rising inflation concerns triggered by the conflict.Last week, the benchmark S&P 500 index recorded its worst quarterly performance since 2022, weighed down by rising energy prices and geopolitical uncertainty since late February.Dimon noted that the US economy remains resilient, with consumers continuing to earn and spend, and businesses staying broadly healthy, though signs of weakening have emerged.He cautioned that economic strength has been supported by significant government deficit spending and past stimulus, while infrastructure investment needs continue to grow.At the same time, he pointed to positives such as fiscal stimulus under President Trump's "Big, Beautiful Bill", deregulation policies and rising capital expenditure driven by artificial intelligence.On financial stability, Dimon said the $1.8-trillion private credit market "probably" does not pose a systemic risk, despite investor concerns and recent withdrawals from such funds.However, he warned that in a downturn, losses across leveraged lending could exceed expectations as credit standards have weakened.Private credit markets also lack transparency and rigorous valuation benchmarks, increasing the risk of investor exits if conditions worsen, he said.Separately, Dimon criticised revised US capital rules, including Basel III and GSIB surcharge norms, calling aspects of the proposals "nonsensical" and "very flawed".He said JPMorgan’s GSIB surcharge would fall only to 5.0%, a level he described as “absurd” and “un-American”, arguing it penalises the bank’s scale and performance.
Article Summary: JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon warns Iran war could trigger oil and commodity price shocks. He also highlighted broader geopolitical risks, including the war in Ukraine, tensions in the Middle East and friction with China. Dimon criticised revised US capital rules, including Basel III and GSIB surcharge norms, calling aspects of the proposals "nonsensical"

Article Title: How selling Alaska in 1867 was a costly mistake for Russia
Article Text:
Image: Wikipedia

Why Russia agreed to the Alaska purchase

The hidden wealth Russia gave away

Strategic and geopolitical consequences







In 1867, Russia’s decision to sell Alaska to America turned out to be a historic mistake that was to go unnoticed for years to come. The sum paid by the US was only $7,200,000. Although, in the light of the circumstances at the time, it appeared logical, retrospectively, the act looks extremely shortsighted. Russia found itself economically constrained, geographically far away from the region and fearful of its falling into the hands of the British without any compensation. In reality, however, the region had proved immensely valuable both strategically and naturally. Its vast reserves of oil, gold, and natural resources would later transform it into one of the most economically significant territories in the United States.The choice of selling Alaska was based on economic and political reasons. By the middle of the 19th century, the Russian Empire experienced financial difficulties as a result of the expensive Crimean War. It had become increasingly hard for the country to manage such remote and thinly populated areas as Alaska.According to EBSCO , “the Russian-American Company was in decline, and the colony failed to yield any profit”.In addition to this, the lucrative fur trade had declined, making the territory much less economically valuable for the empire.At the same time, Russia was afraid that Britain might capture Alaska in case another war broke out between the two countries. Selling the colony to the friendly United States appeared as a logical step. As the Office of the Historian states, William H. Seward, U.S. Secretary of State, “it was a chance to expand its influence in North America and hinder further growth of Britain”.For Russia, the transaction was a chance to minimise the losses. However, it greatly undervalued the potential of Alaska.Another thing that Russia did not expect was the amount of riches that Alaska possessed. Within several decades after the acquisition, the state saw discoveries of enormous deposits of gold, oil, and other minerals.“Alaska has produced more than 40 million ounces of gold,” according to the US Geological Survey. Furthermore, Alaska is home to some of the world's most significant undeveloped mineral deposits, according to the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) . But even more importantly, the state's oil deposits turned out to be extremely valuable. The Prudhoe Bay oil field was discovered in 1968 and became one of North America's biggest.In retrospect, the amount paid by the Americans, $7.2 million, or two cents per acre, was rather laughable. According to Howard I. Kushner , the deal “robbed Russia of an enormously rich territory, the true value of which would only be recognised in the twentieth century.”In addition to resources, there is the question of strategy and how important Alaska is to the United States from a strategic standpoint. This is a very good place when it comes to defence strategy. It borders on the Arctic Ocean and also happens to be close to Russia.During the period of the Cold War, the Alaskan region became a frontier area for the United States, becoming home to military facilities and warning systems.According to Col. Michael J. Forsyth, U.S. Army, the closeness of Russia and Alaska, only about ninety kilometres apart across the Bering Strait, meant that this region became highly significant to the United States' defence plans.From today's point of view, the strategic location of Alaska makes the state very important to the policies of the U.S. related to the Arctic, energy security, and even environmental policies.Thinking back, what seems clear to us now is that the Russians sold Alaska due to immediate needs rather than future considerations. In order to solve the problem, they lost a valuable resource for Russia.Conclusively, the Alaska Purchase should not have been done as it has had a significant impact that will remain throughout history.
Article Summary: Russia sold Alaska to the U.S. in 1867 for only $7,200,000. The sale was based on economic and political reasons. However, the region proved immensely valuable both strategically and naturally. Alaska is home to some of the world's most significant undeveloped mineral deposits. The Prudhoe Bay oil field was discovered in 1968.

Article Title: AI data centers are causing 'stress' not just to tech companies, but also private insurers
Article Text:
Massive investments, growing risks



Unique challenges for data centre insurance companies





AI data centers are reportedly putting new pressure on the insurance industry. According to a CNBC report, experts warn that the rapid growth of these projects is becoming a “stress test” not just for tech companies, but also for private insurers and lenders. Consulting firm McKinsey estimates that global spending on data centers could reach $7 trillion by 2030. As costs rise, tech giants are increasingly relying on private equity, private credit and debt to fund these projects. The scale of investments is creating challenges for insurers.“When you put $10 to $20 billion plus in a single location, it creates capacity issues in the marketplace,” said Tom Harper, data center leader at insurance broker Gallagher as quoted in the CNBC report.He added that the sector has been a “real stress test” over the last few years, as insurers struggle to provide coverage for such large and complex projects. While these facilities are seen as high-quality assets, Harper said insuring them has become difficult due to their size and risk concentration.Private data center deals have crossed $10 billion regularly, with some reaching as high as $40 billion. Companies including Nvidia, Microsoft and BlackRock have already taken part in large investment deals.Rajat Rana, partner at Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan, described the scale as unprecedented. “We’re talking about trillions of dollars, and almost going back to the same cycle where there’s almost no transparency about the financing structures — the scale is astronomical,” he said. He added that the boom in AI data centers could be “the largest peacetime investment project in human history.”Experts say the way these projects are financed is adding another layer of risk. Much of the funding is happening through complex and less transparent debt structures. Rana warned that this trend feels like “deja vu,” referring to past financial cycles. He said the lack of transparency could create risks for investors, insurers and pension funds.As stated in the CNBC report, data centers offer unique challenges due to their mix of real estate and advanced technology. Insurers are now designing custom policies to manage these risks. However, challenges remain. Large facilities are often located in areas prone to extreme weather, and they store expensive equipment, sometimes even before installation.Another key concern is the mismatch between the lifespan of data centers and the hardware they use. While data centers can last decades, GPUs — the chips powering AI — typically last around seven years. Rana described this as a “GPU debt treadmill,” saying, “This is almost like a treadmill that these AI data centers are running on.”He explained that companies may need to keep raising debt to upgrade infrastructure as technology evolves.
Article Summary: Private data center deals have crossed $10 billion regularly, with some reaching as high as $40 billion. McKinsey estimates that global spending on data centers could reach $7 trillion by 2030. Tech giants are increasingly relying on private equity, private credit and debt to fund these projects.

Article Title: 2 lemurs smuggled from Tanzania in flyers’ bags, sent back
Article Text: Navi Mumbai: Two lemurs smuggled from Tanzania in two flyers' baggage were rescued at Mumbai airport on Monday. Customs officials and Wildlife Crime Control Bureau arrested the passengers. The lemurs were medically examined and sent back in 24 hours.Provisions of Wildlife Protection Act, 1972, have been invoked.Pawan Sharma of Resqink Association for Wildlife Welfare (RAWW), an NGO which assisted with the animals' safekeeping, said they were smuggled on an Ethiopian Airways flight.The two were among a growing list of trafficked ‘exotic animals' rescued at Mumbai airport in recent times. Sunish Subramanian Kunju, honorary wildlife warden and founder of Plant and Animals Welfare Society (PAWS), Mumbai, said Chennai airport used to be traffickers' preferred choice for entry into India but they changed tack and turned to the Mumbai around five years ago after air intelligence units in south India tightened vigilance.Numbers attest to the crisis: In 2025, 900 such animals were seized at Mumbai airport and 29 from Bangkok, including melanistic squirrels, ball pythons and iguana, were rescued last month alone. Of the 900, over 80 were found dead. "They are packed in bags with poor ventilation. Many die due to asphyxiation or shock," said Sharma.Experts said a rising demand for ‘trophy pets' has boosted the trafficking trade in India. Macaws can go for Rs 2 lakh, while anacondas and ball pythons command much higher rates.Kunju said flyers caught with such animals are often mere mules and a crackdown on masterminds as well as buyers can help curtail trafficking.
Article Summary: Customs officials and Wildlife Crime Control Bureau arrested the passengers. The lemurs were medically examined and sent back in 24 hours. In 2025, 900 such animals were seized at Mumbai airport and 29 from Bangkok. Of the 900, over 80 were found dead.

Article Title: Bombay high court: Can’t deduct interest on housing loan from accident victim’s income when calculating payout for kin under Motor Vehicles Act
Article Text:
Bombay high court has held that while awarding compensation in accident cases under the Motor Vehicles Act, interest paid on a housing loan should not subtracted from the victim’s income to determine payout amount to kin

Mumbai:

JudgeSpeak

Bombay high court recently held that while awarding compensation in accident cases under the Motor Vehicles (MV) Act, interest paid on a housing loan — allowed as deduction under the Income-Tax (I-T) Act — should not subtracted from the victim’s income to determine payout amount to kin. Nor can a ‘loss’ under the I-T Act determine “just compensation” under the MV Act, Justice Jitendra Jain held.A victim’s income forms the basis of a compensation under the MV Act and such earning is only from either a salary, if employed, or from a business or profession, if self-employed, Justice Jain observed. The July 10 ruling on the interplay of I-T Act and salutary MV Act paved way for Rs 17 lakh more as compensation to a Pune doctor who, 20 years ago, at age 26, became a widow with two minors.The legal issues that cropped up and decided for the first time, as observed by the HC, was over whether ‘loss’ under the I-T Act from property and interest paid on housing loan could be factored in to calculate what the income was. Justice Jain held, “Loss as per the I-T Act under the head “House Property” cannot be set-off against income under the head “Business or Profession” for the purposes of the MV Act.’’ The HC rejected the insurer’s submissions that the loss ought to be set off.Justice Jain said if the insurer’s argument is accepted, it may lead to no payout, as the loss is permitted under the I-T Act to be offset for upto seven years and may even lead to a ‘nil’ income.In 2014, the widow and her two children had challenged a 2012 order of a Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT). She had, in December 2005, lost her doctor husband Bhupendra Kothadia. A speeding truck driven in a wrong direction hit his car and he died on the spot. Seven years on, the tribunal awarded her close to Rs 17 lakh after deducting the interest he paid on a home loan and calculating his income by setting-off of loss under the head ‘Income from House Property’ against positive income under the head ‘Income from Business’.“The set-off provision is for the purpose of determining lower tax, but same cannot be read to lower the compensation under the MV Act,’’ said Justice Jain.Her lawyers. Amit Singh and Abhay Nevgi. argued that the income payable for housing loan was part of victim’s income and only permissible exclusions are income tax and profession tax. But advocate Amol Gatne, for the insurer, argued that home loan interest must be excluded as only then the “disposable income’’ is seen.Claim cases abound and similar issues are pending in other matters, hence the HC also heard four amicus curiae — T J Mendon, Yogesh Pande, D S Joshi and Karishma Jhaveri — who submitted that “on this issue there is not a single decision of any court”.The MV Act is a socio-welfare law, the HC said, and reasoned that if insurer’s contention is accepted, “it would amount to reducing the compensation’’ payable. The HC held that interest payable on a home loan is to be included in the victim’s income.The HC reasoned, “Even if it is accepted that on death full repayment of loan becomes due and payable, still to repay that loan, interest has to be factored in awarding compensation because had the person not died interest liability would continue,’’ but clarified: “This, however, should not be construed to mean that insurance company undertakes to bear repayment liability, but certainly while awarding compensation the interest liability should be added to the compensation award for determining income. ”The HC said any income from house or property or income from capital gains or income from other sources, which continue to be earned even after the death, cannot form the basis to decide the compensation.The widow and two children had sought enhancement of compensation.The Tribunal had directed the insurer, United India Insurance Company Ltd, to pay interest from 2008 when it was added as party. The HC ruled that the insurer must pay from 2006, when the claim for compensation was filed.
