The MV Act requires an insurer to pay from claim plea date, said Justice Jain and knowledge of claim cannot be the criterion.* When it comes to awarding compensation under the MV Act, it is only income from salary or income from business or profession which should be considered for compensating the dependents* Section 71 of the I-T Act provides that if there is a loss under one head, it can be set-off against income from another head. The aim is to make the person liable to pay tax on net income. This provision cannot be borrowed mechanically for the purpose of arriving at “just compensation” under the MV Act. Therefore, the Tribunal’s approach to adopt the taxable income after set-off of loss under property loan, to calculate compensation is erroneous* If interest on loan excluded from income, the dependent would have insufficient funds to service the interest, which otherwise would have been, had the vehicular accident victim not died. Hence for compensation under MV Act, interest on housing loan should not be reduced
Article Summary: Bombay high court recently held that while awarding compensation in accident cases under the Motor Vehicles (MV) Act, interest paid on a housing loan should not subtracted from the victim’s income. The July 10 ruling paved way for Rs 17 lakh more as compensation to a Pune doctor who, 20 years ago, at age 26, became a widow with two minors.

Article Title: HC tells BMC to clear Charkop pond choked with garbage
Article Text:
The judges also directed the collector “to initiate immediate steps for removal of the encroachments”

Mumbai:

Bombay high court on Tuesday directed BMC to undertake an extensive drive for clearing garbage from a pond in Sector 7, Charkop, Kandivli West and cleaning up the area.The direction by Acting Chief Justice Ravindra Ghuge and Justice Gautam Ankhad followed after BMC submitted photographs “as of today” showing the status of the pond due to garbage dumped from slums next to it. “It will shock the conscience of any prudent person as it depicts that the natural pond is virtually blocked,” the judges said. Referring to one particular photograph, they directed that a picture of the site from the same angle be produced before them after two weeks.HC was hearing a 2021 petition for permission to carry out beautification and conservation of the pond due to the presence of mangroves. On July 14, BMC said it wants to put up a gabion wall to stop encroachments. HC then said that it must remove the encroachments first and that the municipal commissioner must take a “definite and firm stand”.BMC’s advocate, Saket Mone, said the encroachments are on collector land. On May 8, the civic body had written to the deputy collector (encroachments) to take action against a slum of nearly 100-150 huts, he said. Mone added that on June 18, a reminder was sent offering to provide whatever resources required for demolition.He said BMC “wants to create a pathway and a plantation and a gabion wall on the edge”. “It will help with the ingress of saline water for the mangroves. ” After Mone submitted photographs of the pond, the judges remarked that they are “shocking”.Advocate Karl Tamboly, for Bombay Environmental Action Group, whose PIL prompted HC in Sept 2018 to mandate taking its permission for projects within the 50m buffer zone of mangroves, said the photographs “are worse than photographs which had earlier come in”.The judges allowed the govt pleader’s request for two weeks to take instructions. They said in the meantime, BMC must undertake a clearance drive. “We would not tolerate any garbage in that natural pond,” they added. The judges also directed the collector “to initiate immediate steps for removal of the encroachments”.Adjourning the hearing to Aug 6, they noted that garbage and encroachments “are two issues which can be said to be the root cause of the disease which is caused to this natural pond and in effect is hurting the mangroves”.
Article Summary: HC was hearing a 2021 petition for permission to carry out beautification and conservation of the pond due to the presence of mangroves. “It will shock the conscience of any prudent person as it depicts that the natural pond is virtually blocked,” judges said.

Article Title: No more wet lease ops? BEST eyes Rs 6,500cr funds to revive own fleet with 5,000 buses in Mumbai; 10,000 over 3 years
Article Text: Mumbai: BEST is preparing for a major shift in its bus operations, with plans to gradually move away from dependence on wet-lease buses and build a fleet of 5,000 self-owned vehicles, including a large electric bus component.Speaking to media after a panel meeting on Tuesday, BEST general manager Sonia Sethi said the undertaking currently has more wet-lease buses than BEST-owned vehicles as its own fleet has steadily shrunk over the years. “However, in the long term, BEST plans to own 5,000 e-buses and expand its overall fleet to 10,000 buses over the next two to three years to meet the mobility needs of Mumbai’s growing population,” she said.To achieve the target of 5,000 owned buses, BEST will require funding of around Rs 6,500 crore. Sethi said a decision on the funding mechanism will be taken at an upcoming BEST committee meeting. The undertaking will examine whether the project should be financed through govt support or by raising its own resources.The move follows directions from deputy chief minister Eknath Shinde and is aimed at restoring the operating model that BEST followed for decades.The self-owned buses will account for half of the fleet.BEST currently operates around 2,800 buses and is expected to receive 1,500 wet-lease electric buses under the Centre’s PM e-Bus initiative, which provides financial support until 2027. Beyond that period, the undertaking intends to focus on acquiring its own buses rather than relying on private operators.Sethi further said that BEST also plans to revive the traditional operating model under which the undertaking owned and maintained its buses and employed its own drivers. According to Sethi, cost-cutting measures introduced in 2019, including a recruitment freeze and increased dependence on wet-lease buses, were adopted to reduce financial pressure and secure support from the BMC. “We now want to reverse many of those decisions,” she said.Signalling a shift away from its dependence on wet-lease operations, Sethi now announced plans to recruit its own bus drivers as part of a broader strategy to gradually rebuild a predominantly self-owned fleet. She said special funds will be earmarked for fresh recruitments, allowing the undertaking to return to its earlier model of operating buses with its own trained workforce. Sethi said the move aims to revive a system that had served Mumbai efficiently for decades, when BEST-owned buses were operated by permanent drivers and were known for better maintenance, safety standards and service reliability. Officials say they believe the decision will help strengthen accountability, improve driver discipline and enhance commuter confidence as BEST expands its fleet in the coming years.The announcement comes amid scrutiny following a series of recent bus accidents. Officials said the objective is to restore public confidence in BEST by improving fleet quality, driver discipline, safety and reliability while creating a modern, largely electric public transport system for Mumbai.
Article Summary: BEST currently operates around 2,800 buses and is expected to receive 1,500 wet- lease electric buses under the Centre’s PM e-Bus initiative. The self-owned buses will account for half of the fleet. To achieve the target of 5,000 owned buses, BEST will require funding of around Rs 6,500 crore.

Article Title: 8 years after losing 3 fingers, toe in ‘fatka robbery’, victim gets Rs 4.2L in Mumbai
Article Text:
The Railway Claims Tribunal awarded Dravita Singh Rs 4.2 lakh compensation for injuries sustained in a 2018 robbery.

The incident

The 2018 incident

MUMBAI: Eight years after Kalyan resident Dravita Singh lost part of her limbs in a horrific instance of ‘fatka robbery’, the Railway Claims Tribunal (RCT) has allowed her claim partly and asked Central Railway to pay her Rs 4.2 lakh, plus interest, as compensation. While welcoming RCT’s order, Singh’s father, Hemant Kumar , pointed out that the trial before the sessions court, however, was yet to start.On Feb 7, 2018, Singh, then 23, had fallen off a moving local train between Sandhurst Road and Masjid stations after being hit by a stick-wielding robber. Her three fingers, a toe and a portion of her right foot were crushed after another train hit her. She underwent multiple surgeries and skin grafts before she could walk again.Singh, through her lawyer C K Sable, filed a claim before the RCT in July 2018. She stepped into the witness box and filed an affidavit, recounting the sequence of events and how she suffered injuries. She also stated that she had a valid return-journey ticket and was a bonafide passenger.The Railways’ counsel opposed her claim by producing the divisional railway manager’s report, which concluded that the incident had occurred when Singh was speaking on her phone while standing on the footboard of the train, which is an offence.The Railways’ counsel also argued that police had not found any ticket in her possession. The counsel argued that as per the motorman and train manager’s statements, Singh was knocked down by an oncoming train while running on the tracks, which amounts to trespassing and is an offence.The RCT, however, observed that “falling off a train and running behind the thief to recover a mobile and, in that process, if the applicant (Singh) sustained injuries, even if she had been infringing railway lines and was hit by a train coming from the opposite direction, it cannot be called trespassing. It is an ‘untoward incident’ defined under the Railway Act”. The RCT also observed that Singh was a bonafide passenger based on her affidavit.Singh was awarded Rs 4.2 lakh as compensation, plus 9% interest, from the date of the incident to the date of the order. Of the amount, Singh was permitted to withdraw Rs 40,000 through her savings account and the rest was to be invested in an annuity deposit scheme in a nationalised bank.Refusing to be bogged down by the traumatic incident, Singh ran the Mumbai Marathon and appeared for her graduation exam with her leg in bandages within a year. Currently, she works with a housing finance company and is simultaneously pursuing an MBA.“We were expecting the trial to be fast-tracked in the sessions court, but eight years have passed and it has not even started. Police have not been in touch with us about the status of the case,” lamented her father.Of the three accused booked in the case, one was a juvenile, while another is out on bail.* Dravita Singh, then 23, fell off a running local train between Sandhurst Road and Masjid stations after a stick-wielding robber hit her to rob her phone on February 7, 2018. She saw another train approaching and tried to roll away from the tracks, but three fingers of her left hand, her toe and a portion of her right foot were crushed by that train. She also suffered a head injury and had to undergo multiple surgeries and skin grafts* CSMT Government Railway Police registered a case of attempt to commit murder and robbery under the Indian Penal Code* Police booked three persons: a 17-year-old boy who had struck Singh; Ajay Pal, who was present when the robbery occurred and helped sell Singh’s stolen phone; and Rukhsar Barbiya, a homemaker from Masjid who purchased the handset. Barbiya is out on bail* Trial yet to start
Article Summary: Dravita Singh lost part of her limbs in a horrific instance of 'fatka robbery' Eight years later, the Railway Claims Tribunal has allowed her claim partly and asked Central Railway to pay her Rs 4.2 lakh, plus interest, as compensation. Singh, then 23, had fallen off a moving local train after being hit by a stick- wielding robber.

Article Title: ‘Gross abuse of the process of law’: HC imposes Rs 10,000 costs on Jalgaon Peoples Co-operative Bank for repeat litigation
Article Text:
Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar:

Bombay high court’s Aurangabad bench has pulled up The Jalgaon Peoples Cooperative Bank Limited for re-litigating an issue already settled against it, dismissing the bank’s writ petition and imposing costs of Rs 10,000 for what it termed a “gross abuse of the process of law”.Justice Sachin Deshmukh observed that the bank had attempted to reopen a controversy on which it had already suffered adverse orders, leading to needless consumption of valuable judicial time. “The conduct of the petitioner bank in re-litigating an issue on which it has already suffered adverse orders clearly amounts to a gross abuse of the process of law. This court cannot countenance a public financial institution pursuing unnecessary litigation and costs need to be imposed to curtail such frivolous and vexatious litigation. ..” justice Deshmukh observed.The petition had challenged a Nov 11, 2013, order of the commissioner for cooperation and registrar of cooperative societies, Maharashtra, directing the bank to provide documents sought by a borrower who was also a member of the cooperative society.Court records showed the respondent had taken a term loan of Rs 1.10 crore in Nov 2006. After defaulting on repayment, recovery proceedings were initiated under the Maharashtra Cooperative Societies Act, culminating in a recovery certificate issued by the assistant registrar in Nov 2012.Subsequently, the borrower sought copies of the bank’s balance sheets, profit and loss accounts, audit reports, minutes of general body meetings and other records. The bank declined the request, arguing that the borrower’s status as a wilful defaulter suspended his membership rights.The borrower then approached the commissioner for cooperation, who directed the bank to furnish the information. Before the high court, the bank contended that a defaulter loses the right under Section 32 of the Maharashtra Cooperative Societies Act to inspect internal records.Rejecting the argument, justice Deshmukh held that Section 32 granted every member a statutory right to inspect society records and obtain certified copies on payment of prescribed fees. The court ruled that this right could not be denied merely because a member had defaulted on a loan.The court also held that the commissioner had acted within jurisdiction in directing disclosure of the requested documents, including audit reports. Significantly, the high court noted that the same bank had faced an identical issue in an earlier writ petition decided by a division bench on April 8, 2014, which had categorically upheld a member’s right to obtain certified copies of statutory records.Having already suffered an adverse ruling on the issue, the bank could not seek to revive the dispute through fresh proceedings, the court said. Further, terming the petition devoid of merit, the court dismissed it and directed the bank to deposit Rs 10,000 with the HC legal services sub-committee within four weeks.
Article Summary: Bombay high court’s Aurangabad bench has pulled up The Jalgaon Peoples Cooperative Bank Limited for re-litigating an issue already settled against it. Justice Sachin Deshmukh observed that the bank had attempted to reopen a controversy on which it had already suffered adverse orders, leading to needless consumption of valuable judicial time.

Article Title: Around 1kg fake silver jewellery found in donation box at Purushottam temple
Article Text:
Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar:

Nearly one kilogram of imitation silver jewellery was found in the donation box of the renowned Purushottam temple at Purushottampuri in Beed district after jewellers examined offerings made by the devotees during the recently concluded Adhik Maas pilgrimage.Traditionally, devotees donate precious metals at this temple during the Adhik Maas period. The scrutiny of jewellery and ornaments donated at the temple revealed that while devotees offered around 9 grams of gold and 446 grams of genuine silver jewellery, nearly 973 grams of silver-coloured imitation ornaments had also been placed in the donation box.Temple committee chairperson Vijay Golekar said the month-long pilgrimage concluded peacefully with the cooperation of the temple administration, villagers, public representatives, police, health and revenue department officials.“Devotees donated around Rs 1.5 crore to Lord Purushottam. They also offered gold and silver ornaments. However, nearly 973 grams of the silver jewellery was found to be fake during examination," he said.Those associated with the temple administration suspect that the possibility of jewellers selling silver colour polished metals by falsely claiming it to be original silver to the gullible devotees, who ended up donating it to the temple.The pilgrimage, held from May 17 to June 15 during the Adhik Maas period, witnessed an unprecedented turnout with an estimated 50 lakh devotees visiting the temple situated on the banks of the Godavari river in Majalgaon taluka.The temple is considered the country's only shrine dedicated to Lord Purushottam and is associated with the sacred Chakratirth, making a holy dip in the Godavari and darshan of the deity especially significant during Adhik Maas.According to the temple committee, devotees contributed around Rs 1.5 crore in cash during the pilgrimage, in addition to donations of gold and silver ornaments. The committee also noted a substantial rise in footfall compared to the previous Adhik Maas pilgrimage held three years ago.Temple authorities said the discovery of imitation silver ornaments came to light only after the donated jewellery was examined by authorised jewellers following the conclusion of the pilgrimage. No complaint has been registered, and the committee has not indicated whether any further action will be initiated regarding the fake jewellery donations.
Article Summary: Traditionally, devotees donate precious metals at this temple during the Adhik Maas period. The scrutiny of jewellery and ornaments donated at the temple revealed that while devotees offered around 9 grams of gold and 446 grams of genuine silver jewellery.

Article Title: Aurangabad bench of Bombay high court issues notices on plea seeking CBI, ED probe into alleged Rs 3,000 crore investment scam
Article Text:
Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar:

The Aurangabad bench of the Bombay high court on Monday issued notices on a criminal writ petition seeking transfer of the investigation into the alleged Tredz-Infinite Becon-Sispay investment scam — worth over Rs 3,000 crore — to the Central Bureau of Investigation and the Enforcement Directorate (ED).A division bench of Justices Sandipkumar More and Vaishali Patil-Jadhav sought responses from all respondents, including the Union govt, after hearing the petition.The petition has been filed by Oneness for Solution Foundation, a registered society claiming to represent around 3,000 alleged victims of the scam, along with Nitin Gangarde, the original complainant in an FIR registered at the Shrigonda police station in Ahilyanagar district.Appearing for the petitioners, advocates Ashish Sonawane and Vilas Sonawane told the court that at least eight FIRs have been registered at different police stations across Maharashtra in connection with the alleged fraud and that nearly 1.3 lakh investors are believed to have been duped.According to the petition, the accused masterminds of the massive Ponzi scam allegedly operated a vast network comprising partnership firms, LLPs, private limited companies and a multi-state credit cooperative society, promising monthly returns of 8-10% through investments in the capital market.The plea further alleges that investors were lured by using the name of Tredz Investment Private Limited, a SEBI-, NSE- and BSE-registered entity. It refers to a Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) adjudication order under which the regulator allegedly cancelled the company’s registration and imposed a penalty of Rs 1 crore after recording the alleged modus operandi.The petitioners have also questioned the manner of the ongoing investigation. They contended that while provisions of the Banning of Unregulated Deposit Schemes (BUDS) Act have been invoked in cases registered in Pune and Ahilyanagar, the investigation has not been transferred to the CBI as envisaged under the law. The plea further alleges that partial chargesheets filed in some cases indicate serious lapses in the probe.The petition additionally claims that the proceeds of the alleged fraud were laundered through banking channels and that some accused have acquired foreign citizenship or overseas residency while allegedly transferring funds abroad through offshore entities and cryptocurrencies.Seeking intervention by central agencies, the petition has urged the HC to direct an ED investigation under the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA), initiate action against fugitive accused under relevant laws, and hand over the overall investigation to the CBI.
Article Summary: The petition has been filed by Oneness for Solution Foundation, a registered society claiming to represent around 3,000 alleged victims of the scam. A division bench of Justices Sandipkumar More and Vaishali Patil-Jadhav sought responses from all respondents, including the Union govt, after hearing the petition.

Article Title: HC refuses to revive delayed divorce appeal after ex-husband’s remarriage
Article Text:
Bombay high court’s Aurangabad bench

Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar

:

Bombay HC’s Aurangabad bench has held that once the statutory period for challenging a divorce decree expires and a former spouse legally remarries, a delayed appeal against the divorce becomes infructuous and condoning the delay serves no useful purpose.A division bench of Justice Sandipkumar More and Justice Vaishali Patil-Jadhav dismissed a woman’s plea seeking condonation of a 381-day delay in filing an appeal against a family court decree dissolving her marriage. The court also rejected the proposed appeal, holding that it had become infructuous from the outset as the woman’s former husband had contracted a valid second marriage after the limitation period for filing an appeal had lapsed.The bench observed that under Section 15 of the Hindu Marriage Act and Section 19 of the Family Courts Act, either spouse was entitled to remarry if no appeal was filed within the prescribed limitation period. Once that period expires, the divorce decree attains finality, enabling the parties to lawfully enter into another marriage, it observed.The woman had contended that she became aware of the divorce decree only during proceedings under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act.She argued that her former husband’s subsequent remarriage could not extinguish her statutory right to appeal and sought support from principles applied in service law and the doctrine of lis pendens.Rejecting the argument, the court held that principles governing service matters and property disputes were not applicable to matrimonial litigation. “The reliance placed on principles from Service Law or the doctrine of lis pendens under Section 52 of the Transfer of Property Act are inapplicable to the facts of the present case,” the bench observed.Relying on the Supreme Court’s decision in Krishnaveni Rai vs Pankaj Rai and an earlier Bombay HC judgment, the bench held that a second marriage solemnised after the expiry of the appeal period remained valid and could not be invalidated merely because a delayed appeal was filed later. While the right to appeal was a substantive right, it remained subject to the law of limitation, the bench said.The court also found the woman’s explanation for the delay unconvincing, noting that she had participated in the divorce proceedings by filing a written statement but subsequently chose to remain absent. It further clarified that dismissal of the appeal would not bar the woman from independently pursuing a claim for permanent alimony before the appropriate forum.
Article Summary: Bombay HC’s Aurangabad bench has held that once the statutory period for challenging a divorce decree expires and a former spouse legally remarries, a delayed appeal against the divorce becomes infructuous. The court also rejected the proposed appeal, holding that it had become inf Structuous from the outset.

Article Title: Sena consolidates hold on cooperative sector with DCC Bank wins in Sambhajinagar & Parbhani
Article Text: Shiv Sena MLA Vilas Bhumre was on Monday unanimously elected chairperson of the Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar District Central Cooperative (DCC) Bank
Article Summary: Shiv Sena MLA Vilas Bhumre was on Monday unanimously elected chairperson of the Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar District Central Cooperative (DCC) Bank. He is a member of Shiv Sena's state executive committee.

Article Title: Police crack down on mule accounts; 14 FIRs filed in Sambhajinagar city, Latur dist over Rs 1.82-crore suspicious transactions
Article Text:
Police have urged citizens not to share bank account details, ATM cards, cheque books, internet banking credentials or UPI information with anyone

Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar:

Police have launched a major crackdown on mule bank accounts used by cyber fraud syndicates, registering 14 FIRs in Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar city and Latur district after tracing suspicious transactions worth more than Rs 1.82 crore.In Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar, the city police on Friday registered four FIRs against alleged mule account holders accused of knowingly allowing organised cyber crime networks to use their bank accounts to receive, transfer and withdraw funds obtained through online scams. Investigators traced suspicious transactions exceeding Rs 51.9 lakh. The Latur district police have registered 10 cases after tracking over Rs 1.3 crore in suspected cyber fraud proceeds routed through bank accounts operated in the district.Mule accounts are bank accounts opened or handed over to third parties in exchange for commissions and are routinely used by fraudsters to route illicit funds and obscure the money trail. “Cyber fraud networks cannot operate without mule bank accounts that receive and move cheated money. We are identifying such account holders through scientific analysis of money trails on the NCRP and Samnvaya portals and taking stringent legal action against those found to have knowingly facilitated cyber criminals,” Sambhajinagar city police commissioner Pravin Pawar said, urging citizens not to rent, sell or permit the misuse of their bank accounts.The largest case was registered at the Mukundwadi police station. According to the FIR filed by cyber police constable Shailesh Gangaram Adbilla, the accused’s the account was linked to nine cyber fraud complaints from different parts of the country with identified suspicious credits of Rs 32.74 lakh during Oct 13-15, 2025.The Jawahar Nagar police registered three other FIRs involving alleged cyber fraud proceeds of Rs 6.84 lakh, Rs 6.80 lakh and Rs 5.53 lakh against residents of Paithan, Harsul and Katkat Gate, respectively. All the four cases have been registered under sections 112(2), 318(4), 317(2), 61(2) and 3(5) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS).In Latur, five of the 10 FIRs were registered at the Gandhi Chowk police station, where investigators traced suspicious transactions worth Rs 1.24 crore. Two cases involving Rs 53,300 were registered at the Shivajinagar police station, while the Renapur police booked two cases linked to Rs 4.89 lakh. One case involving Rs 31,000 was registered at the Ahmedpur police station.Superintendent of police Amol Tambe said mule accounts were a critical link in cyber-enabled financial crimes and warned citizens against sharing banking facilities for monetary benefits. “We are taking stringent action against mule account holders because such accounts are a vital link in cyber financial crimes. People should never allow others to use their bank accounts or share banking credentials for monetary gain. Those found facilitating cyber fraud will face strict legal action,” he said.Investigators found that while victims were spread across multiple states, the defrauded money was deposited into bank accounts based in Latur district. Cases were subsequently registered by local police stations based on the location of the beneficiary accounts.Police said account holders were often lured with 5-10% commission. In several cases, individuals allegedly provide access to their bank accounts, ATM cards, cheque books, internet banking credentials and OTPs, while some sell bank accounts outright to cyber criminals.Officers warned that those whose accounts are used for cyber fraud could face arrest, prosecution, account freezes and long-term damage to their financial credibility, affecting access to loans and other banking services.Police have urged citizens not to share bank account details, ATM cards, cheque books, internet banking credentials or UPI information with anyone and to report suspicious activity immediately through the cybercrime helpline 1930 or the nearest police station.
Article Summary: Police have launched a major crackdown on mule bank accounts used by cyber fraud syndicates. They have registered 14 FIRs in Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar city and Latur district after tracing suspicious transactions worth more than Rs 1.82 crore. Mule accounts are bank accounts opened or handed over to third parties in exchange for commissions and are routinely used by fraudsters.

Article Title: 35,000-seat cricket stadium planned near Kolhapur
Article Text:
Located about 15 km from Kolhapur city, the proposed complex will be developed as a multi-sport hub rather than a cricket-only venue.

Kolhapur

:

A 12.76-hectare plot at Vikaswadi MIDC near Kagal has been transferred by the Maharashtra government to the Kolhapur District Cricket Association (KDCA) for the development of an international cricket stadium.The memorandum of understanding (MoU) was signed on Tuesday between the state sports department and the KDCA. Former Rajya Sabha MP and KDCA president Sambhajiraje Chhatrapati signed the agreement on behalf of the association, which will now be responsible for raising funds and executing the project.Located about 15 km from Kolhapur city, the proposed complex will be developed as a multi-sport hub rather than a cricket-only venue. Besides the stadium, it will include facilities for boxing, judo, archery, wrestling, swimming, table tennis, fencing and other sports. A cricket museum will also be built, with the project expected to generate employment opportunities for local residents.The cricket stadium, with a seating capacity of around 35,000, will be built in accordance with BCCI and ICC norms. The project will feature a modern pavilion, practice grounds, an indoor training centre, a sports science centre, a clubhouse, floodlights, a cricket museum, parking facilities for players, officials and spectators, internal roads, drainage systems, a solar power project and advanced security infrastructure.The demand for an international cricket stadium in Kolhapur had been pursued for years by veteran cricketers Bal Patankar and Ruturaj Ingale, who sought land for the project. In September 2024, Sambhajiraje Chhatrapati took up the proposal with then deputy chief minister Ajit Pawar , following which the government approved the transfer of land from MIDC to the KDCA.The MoU was signed on Tuesday, on the eve of Ajit Pawar’s birth anniversary, in the presence of chief minister Devendra Fadnavis and deputy chief minister Sunetra Pawar.Sambhajiraje said construction of the stadium would begin soon and that the facility would be built to international standards with an eye on the future of the sport.“The stadium will create new opportunities for thousands of aspiring cricketers in western Maharashtra and the Konkan region. Hosting national and international cricket competitions, providing high-quality training facilities, and promoting sports tourism will boost the local economy significantly. By raising the necessary funds and completing all processes transparently and with proper planning, we are determined to build a world-class cricket stadium for Kolhapur as soon as possible,” he said.Ministers Prakash Abitkar, Hasan Mushrif and Shivendraraje Bhosale, along with KDCA office-bearers, were present at the MoU signing ceremony in Mumbai.
Article Summary: A 12.76-hectare plot at Vikaswadi MIDC near Kagal has been transferred by the Maharashtra government to the Kolhapur District Cricket Association. Besides the stadium, it will include facilities for boxing, judo, archery, wrestling, swimming, table tennis, fencing and other sports.

Article Title: Fraud worth Rs 61.7 crore uncovered at Sant Balumama temple, 2 booked; probe handed over to Economic Offences Wing
Article Text:
The Balumama temple at Adamapur is a prominent pilgrimage centre in the region, drawing lakhs of devotees from Maharashtra and Karnataka

Kolhapur

:

An alleged fraud involving Rs 61.7 crore at the Sant Sadguru Shri Balumama Devalaya Trust in Adamapur, Kolhapur, has led to the registration of a criminal case against two — the trust’s former working president and secretary. The investigation has now been handed over to the Economic Offences Wing.Superintendent of police Neelotpal said the allegations include the purchase and sale of temple land using forged documents and misuse of trust funds. “Given the magnitude of the case, the probe was transferred to the Economic Offences Wing (EOW),” he said.The case was registered at the Bhudargad police station on a complaint filed by Rangrao Gadkari, an inspector from the charity commissioner’s Mumbai office. According to the complaint, multiple petitions were filed in the Bombay high court alleging financial irregularities in the management of the trust, prompting a detailed inquiry.Investigations revealed irregularities worth nearly Rs 30 crore between March 2020 and March 2026 in transactions involving trust-owned land and the valuation shown while selling Balumama’s flock of 44,000 sheep. The HC had constituted an inquiry committee to examine the allegations.Deputy superintendent of police Suvarna Patki, of the EOW said, “Important documents from the Devasthan committee have been seized, and the scrutiny of the documents is currently on. The investigation will give clarity on the matter. Strict action will be taken against those found guilty.”The Balumama temple at Adamapur is a prominent pilgrimage centre in the region, drawing lakhs of devotees from Maharashtra and Karnataka. The alleged embezzlement in the trust has triggered concern among devotees in both states.Meanwhile, the trust’s former working president denied any involvement in the alleged fraud. “I held the position of the working president of the Devasthan Trust only from May 2024 to July 4, 2025. During this tenure, there was no malpractice. I have no connection with the alleged embezzlement as I was not the working president then. Why was a case registered against only two when there were 21 trustees?” he asked.
Article Summary: Alleged fraud at Sant Sadguru Shri Balumama Devalaya Trust in Adamapur, Kolhapur. The investigation has now been handed over to the Economic Offences Wing. The alleged embezzlement in the trust has triggered concern among devotees in Maharashtra and Karnataka.

Article Title: Increase discharge from Almatti Dam, Sangli irrigation dept urges K’taka govt
Article Text:
Almatti dam is currently 79% full

Kolhapur:

TOI

The Sangli irrigation department has written to the Karnataka govt asking them to keep all gates of the Hippargi Barrage open as the flow of the Krishna river was likely to increase following heavy rainfall received in Sangli, Satara and Kolhapur districts.The department, in the letter, has stated that as Almatti dam was currently 79% full, there‘s a possibility of flooding in villages along the Krishna river in Kolhapur and Sangli districts.“The Karnataka irrigation department should immediately increase the discharge from the Almatti dam and open all gates of the Hippargi barrage on the Krishna river,” read the letter sent to the Karnataka govt on Monday.Seema Mohite, executive engineer of Sangli irrigation department, said, “As per the decision taken during the inter-state coordination meeting, the Karnataka administration has been requested to keep all the gates of Hippargi Barrage fully open so that the natural flow of Krishna river remains uninterrupted during the monsoon. It has also been demanded that the water level of Almatti dam should be maintained between 517 and 517.5 meters till mid-August. ”Currently, the water level in Almatti dam is around 518 meters with 46,848 cusecs inflowing into the dam and 12,500 cusecs being discharged. The dam has a storage capacity of 94.64 TMC and is 79% full.Retired engineer and member of Krishna Flood Control Committee Prabhakar Kengar, while talking to, said, “During the inter-state meeting, both the irrigation departments had decided to maintain the water level of the Almatti dam between 517 and 517.5 meters till mid-Aug.Yet, the water level of the Almatti dam has been kept at 518 meters on July 20 itself. This means that the Karnataka irrigation department has violated the decision taken in the meeting.”Meanwhile, on Tuesday, moderate rainfall continued in the ghat and dam catchment areas of Satara and Kolhapur districts. In Satara, Navaja received 86mm showers, Mahabaleshwar 78mm and Koyna 67mm between Monday and Tuesday.In Kolhapur, Radhanagari dam (107mm), Jambre (139mm) and Sarfnala (122mm) were among the highest rainfall receiving dam catchments on Tuesday.
Article Summary: The Sangli irrigation department has written to the Karnataka govt asking them to keep all gates of the Hippargi Barrage open. The department, in the letter, has stated that as Almatti dam was currently 79% full, there’s a possibility of flooding in villages along the Krishna river in Kolhapur and Sangli districts.
